EVENTS.title: Vikings Reach Newfoundland
EVENTS.description: Vikings from Iceland, who had colonised Greenland, reach Labrador and the island of Newfoundland. Their settlement, L'Anse aux Meadows, is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site — the earliest known European presence in the Americas.

EVENTS.title: John Cabot Maps the East Coast
EVENTS.description: John Cabot, an Italian immigrant to England, becomes the first European to map Canada's East Coast, setting foot on Newfoundland or Cape Breton Island. He claims the "New Founde Land" for England. English settlement does not begin until 1610.

EVENTS.title: Jacques Cartier Names Canada
EVENTS.description: Jacques Cartier makes the first of three voyages (1534–1542) across the Atlantic, claiming the land for King Francis I of France. He explores the St. Lawrence River and sets eyes on present-day Québec City and Montreal. He hears two guides speak the Iroquoian word kanata ("village") — by the 1550s the name "Canada" appears on maps.

EVENTS.title: First European Settlement: Port-Royal
EVENTS.description: The first European settlement north of Florida is established by French explorers Pierre de Monts and Samuel de Champlain — first on St. Croix Island, then at Port-Royal in Acadia (present-day Nova Scotia). The descendants of these colonists are the Acadians.

EVENTS.title: Champlain Founds Québec City
EVENTS.description: Samuel de Champlain builds a fortress at what is now Québec City. He allies the colony with the Algonquin, Montagnais and Huron against the Iroquois Confederacy. Outstanding leaders like Jean Talon, Bishop Laval, and Count Frontenac would later build a French Empire reaching from Hudson Bay to the Gulf of Mexico.

EVENTS.title: Hudson's Bay Company Founded
EVENTS.description: King Charles II of England grants the Hudson's Bay Company exclusive trading rights over the vast watershed draining into Hudson Bay. For the next 100 years the Company competes with Montreal-based traders. French and Aboriginal peoples collaborate in the fur-trade economy driven by demand for beaver pelts in Europe.

EVENTS.title: French–Iroquois Peace
EVENTS.description: After nearly a century of conflict, the French and the Iroquois Confederacy make peace in 1701. This ends a prolonged struggle that had threatened the survival of New France and shaped the early alliances of colonial Canada.

EVENTS.title: The Great Upheaval: Acadian Deportation
EVENTS.description: Between 1755 and 1763, during the war between Britain and France, more than two-thirds of the Acadians are deported from their homeland. Despite this ordeal — known as the "Great Upheaval" — the Acadians survive and maintain their unique identity. Today Acadian culture flourishes, and New Brunswick is Canada's only officially bilingual province.

EVENTS.title: Battle of the Plains of Abraham
EVENTS.description: The British defeat the French at the Battle of the Plains of Abraham at Québec City, marking the end of France's empire in North America. Both commanders — British Brigadier James Wolfe and French Marquis de Montcalm — are killed leading their troops in battle. This decisive battle shapes the future of Canada as a bilingual nation.

EVENTS.title: Royal Proclamation of 1763
EVENTS.description: Following the war, Great Britain renames the colony the "Province of Quebec." The Royal Proclamation of 1763 by King George III first guarantees Aboriginal territorial rights — establishing the basis for negotiating treaties with newcomers, treaties that were not always fully respected.

EVENTS.title: The Quebec Act
EVENTS.description: The British Parliament passes the Quebec Act — one of the constitutional foundations of Canada. It allows religious freedom for Catholics, permits them to hold public office (not then allowed in Britain), and restores French civil law while maintaining British criminal law. It is a landmark in the tradition of accommodation that defines Canada.

EVENTS.title: American Independence
EVENTS.description: The 13 British colonies to the south of Quebec declare independence and form the United States. North America is again divided by war. This event drives tens of thousands of Loyalists northward into Canada and shapes Canada's distinct identity separate from the United States.

EVENTS.title: Loyalists Flee to Canada
EVENTS.description: More than 40,000 people loyal to the Crown — called "Loyalists" — flee the American Revolution to settle in Nova Scotia and Quebec. Joseph Brant leads thousands of Loyalist Mohawk Indians into Canada. About 3,000 Black Loyalists come north seeking a better life. They come from Dutch, German, British, Aboriginal and other backgrounds.

EVENTS.title: Constitutional Act of 1791
EVENTS.description: The Constitutional Act divides the Province of Quebec into Upper Canada (later Ontario) — mainly Loyalist, Protestant and English-speaking — and Lower Canada (later Quebec) — heavily Catholic and French-speaking. Both are granted legislative assemblies elected by the people, the first in Canada. The name "Canada" becomes official.

EVENTS.title: First Steps Toward Abolition of Slavery
EVENTS.description: Upper Canada, led by Lieutenant Governor John Graves Simcoe, becomes the first province in the British Empire to move toward abolition of slavery. In 1807 the British Parliament prohibited buying and selling of slaves, and in 1833 abolished slavery throughout the Empire. Thousands of escaped slaves followed "the North Star" to Canada via the Underground Railroad.

EVENTS.title: Slavery Abolished Throughout the Empire
EVENTS.description: The British Parliament abolishes slavery throughout the Empire. Thousands of escaped slaves from the United States had already settled in Canada via the Underground Railroad — a Christian anti-slavery network. Canada's early steps toward abolition made it a beacon of freedom for those fleeing slavery.

EVENTS.title: War of 1812 Begins
EVENTS.description: Believing it would be easy to conquer Canada, the United States launches an invasion in June 1812. Canadian volunteers, First Nations and British soldiers unite to defend Canada. The Americans are mistaken — Canada successfully repels the invasion, and the war ultimately confirms Canada's independence from the United States.

EVENTS.title: Brock Captures Detroit & Queenston Heights
EVENTS.description: Major-General Sir Isaac Brock captures Detroit but is killed while defending against an American attack at Queenston Heights, near Niagara Falls — a battle the Americans lose. Brock is allied with Shawnee Chief Tecumseh, whose First Nations warriors are vital to Canada's defence. Brock is celebrated as one of Canada's greatest heroes.

EVENTS.title: Laura Secord's Warning
EVENTS.description: Laura Secord, pioneer wife and mother of five, makes a dangerous 19-mile (30 km) journey on foot to warn Lieutenant James FitzGibbon of a planned American attack. Her bravery contributes to victory at the Battle of Beaver Dams. She is recognized as a Canadian heroine to this day.

EVENTS.title: Battle of Châteauguay
EVENTS.description: Lieutenant-Colonel Charles de Salaberry and just 460 soldiers — mostly French Canadians — turn back 4,000 American invaders at Châteauguay, south of Montreal. It is one of the most remarkable defensive victories in Canadian history and a source of enduring pride for French Canada.

EVENTS.title: Americans Burn York (Toronto)
EVENTS.description: In 1813, the Americans burn Government House and the Parliament Buildings in York (now Toronto). HMS Shannon, a Royal Navy frigate, captures the USS Chesapeake and leads it into Halifax harbour. These events escalate the conflict and prompt the British retaliation of 1814.

EVENTS.title: Canada Burns the White House
EVENTS.description: In retaliation for the burning of York, Major-General Robert Ross leads an expedition from Nova Scotia that burns the White House and other public buildings in Washington, D.C. Ross dies in battle shortly after and is buried in Halifax with full military honours.

EVENTS.title: War of 1812 Ends: Canada Remains Free
EVENTS.description: By 1814 the American attempt to conquer Canada has failed. The Duke of Wellington sends his best soldiers to defend Canada and chooses Bytown (Ottawa) as the endpoint of the Rideau Canal. The present-day Canada–U.S.A. border is partly an outcome of this war, which ensured Canada would remain independent.

EVENTS.title: Rebellions of 1837–38
EVENTS.description: Reformers in Upper and Lower Canada believe progress toward full democracy is too slow. Armed rebellions occur outside Montreal and in Toronto; they are defeated by British troops and volunteers. Lord Durham is sent to report on the rebellions and recommends responsible government — and controversially, that French Canadians should assimilate.

EVENTS.title: Responsible Government Achieved
EVENTS.description: Nova Scotia becomes the first British North American colony to attain full responsible government (1847–48). In 1848–49 Lord Elgin introduces it in United Canada. Sir Louis-Hippolyte La Fontaine, champion of French language rights and democracy, becomes the first leader of a responsible government — meaning ministers must hold the confidence of elected representatives.

EVENTS.title: Confederation: Birth of Canada
EVENTS.description: The British Parliament passes the British North America Act. The Dominion of Canada is officially born on July 1, 1867 — with Ontario, Quebec, Nova Scotia and New Brunswick as founding provinces. Sir John A. Macdonald becomes Canada's first Prime Minister. Until 1982 this day was "Dominion Day"; today it is Canada Day.

EVENTS.title: First Riel Uprising
EVENTS.description: When Canada takes over the vast northwest from the Hudson's Bay Company, the 12,000 Métis of the Red River are not consulted. Louis Riel leads an armed uprising and seizes Fort Garry. Ottawa sends soldiers; Riel flees to the United States. Canada establishes a new province: Manitoba (1870). Riel is seen by many as a hero and the father of Manitoba.

EVENTS.title: Manitoba & Northwest Territories Join Canada
EVENTS.description: Following the Red River Uprising, Ottawa establishes Manitoba as a new province in 1870 and takes control of the Northwest Territories. This is a crucial step in building a nation that stretches from sea to sea — though the process displaces Indigenous and Métis peoples from their lands.

EVENTS.title: British Columbia Joins Canada
EVENTS.description: British Columbia joins Canada in 1871 after Ottawa promises to build a railway to the West Coast. This promise leads directly to the construction of the Canadian Pacific Railway, completed in 1885 — a defining national achievement that binds the country from the Atlantic to the Pacific.

EVENTS.title: North West Mounted Police Founded
EVENTS.description: Prime Minister Macdonald establishes the North West Mounted Police (NWMP) in 1873 to maintain order in the West and assist in negotiations with Indigenous nations. The NWMP founds Fort Calgary, Fort MacLeod and other centres. Regina becomes its headquarters. Today, as the RCMP, they are one of Canada's most iconic symbols.

EVENTS.title: Canadian Pacific Railway Completed
EVENTS.description: On November 7, 1885, Donald Smith (Lord Strathcona) drives the last spike of the Canadian Pacific Railway, completing the line from sea to sea. The project is financed by British and American investors and built by both European and Chinese labour. The Chinese are afterward subjected to the discriminatory Head Tax — for which Canada apologised in 2006.

EVENTS.title: Louis Riel Executed
EVENTS.description: As Métis and Indigenous rights are threatened by westward settlement, a second rebellion in 1885 in present-day Saskatchewan leads to Riel's trial and execution for high treason — a decision strongly opposed in Quebec. Gabriel Dumont is the Métis' greatest military leader in this conflict. Riel is seen by many as a hero and father of Manitoba.

EVENTS.title: Manitoba: First Province to Grant Women the Vote
EVENTS.description: Manitoba becomes the first province to grant voting rights to women in 1916. The women's suffrage movement in Canada was founded by Dr. Emily Stowe, the first Canadian woman to practise medicine. This provincial breakthrough paves the way for the federal vote granted in 1917–18.

EVENTS.title: Battle of Vimy Ridge — April 9
EVENTS.description: The Canadian Corps captures Vimy Ridge in April 1917, with 10,000 killed or wounded, securing Canada's reputation as the "shock troops of the British Empire." One Canadian officer said: "It was Canada from the Atlantic to the Pacific on parade… In those few minutes I witnessed the birth of a nation." April 9 is celebrated as Vimy Day.

EVENTS.title: "In Flanders Fields" Written
EVENTS.description: Canadian medical officer Lieutenant-Colonel John McCrae composes the poem "In Flanders Fields" in 1915 — one of the most famous war poems in history. It is recited every Remembrance Day: "In Flanders fields the poppies blow / Between the crosses, row on row…" The red poppy worn on November 11 is inspired by this poem.

EVENTS.title: Women Win the Federal Vote
EVENTS.description: The federal government of Sir Robert Borden gives women the right to vote in federal elections — first to nurses at the front, then to women related to men in active service. In 1918, most Canadian female citizens aged 21 and over receive full voting rights. The suffrage movement was founded by Dr. Emily Stowe.

EVENTS.title: The Hundred Days Offensive
EVENTS.description: Under General Sir Arthur Currie — Canada's greatest soldier — the Canadian Corps advances alongside French and British Empire troops in the final Hundred Days. This includes the victorious Battle of Amiens on August 8, 1918 — which the Germans called "the black day of the German Army" — followed by Arras, Canal du Nord, Cambrai and Mons.

EVENTS.title: Armistice — End of the First World War
EVENTS.description: Germany and Austria surrender; the war ends with the Armistice on November 11, 1918. In total 60,000 Canadians were killed and 170,000 wounded. Every year on November 11 at the 11th hour, Canadians observe Remembrance Day, wearing the red poppy to honour over one million who served and the 110,000 who gave their lives.

EVENTS.title: Agnes Macphail: First Woman MP
EVENTS.description: Agnes Macphail, a farmer and teacher, becomes the first woman elected to the House of Commons in 1921 — a landmark moment following the winning of women's suffrage. She goes on to champion prison reform, pacifism and rural communities throughout her long political career.

EVENTS.title: The Great Depression
EVENTS.description: The stock market crash of 1929 leads to the Great Depression — the "Dirty Thirties." Unemployment reaches 27% by 1933. Farmers in Western Canada are hit hardest by low grain prices and terrible drought. This era drives growing demand for a social safety net, including minimum wages, a standard work week, unemployment insurance and old-age pensions.

EVENTS.title: Bank of Canada Created
EVENTS.description: The Bank of Canada is created in 1934 as a central bank to manage the money supply and bring stability to the financial system. This is a direct response to the economic chaos of the Great Depression and marks a significant expansion of the federal government's role in managing the economy.

EVENTS.title: Canada Joins the Second World War
EVENTS.description: When Adolf Hitler, the Nazi dictator of Germany, invades Poland and conquers much of Europe, Canada joins its democratic allies. More than one million Canadians and Newfoundlanders serve out of a population of 11.5 million — a high proportion — and 44,000 are killed. Canada contributes more to the Allied air effort than any other Commonwealth country.

EVENTS.title: Quebec Women Win the Vote
EVENTS.description: Thanks to the work of Thérèse Casgrain and others, Quebec becomes the last province to grant women the provincial vote in 1940. Casgrain campaigned relentlessly for women's rights and later also led the CCF party in Quebec.

EVENTS.title: Defence of Hong Kong
EVENTS.description: Canadians suffer losses in the unsuccessful defence of Hong Kong from Imperial Japan in 1941. Canadian prisoners of war are grossly maltreated. Japan also invades the Aleutian Islands, attacks a lighthouse on Vancouver Island, and launches fire balloons over B.C. and the Prairies.

EVENTS.title: Raid on Dieppe
EVENTS.description: A large-scale Allied raid on the Nazi-controlled port of Dieppe on the coast of France fails in 1942 with heavy Canadian casualties. Though a costly defeat, the lessons learned at Dieppe directly inform the planning of the successful D-Day invasion two years later.

EVENTS.title: D-Day: Canadians Storm Juno Beach
EVENTS.description: In the epic invasion of Normandy on June 6, 1944, 15,000 Canadian troops storm and capture Juno Beach from the German Army. Approximately one in ten Allied soldiers on D-Day is Canadian. The Canadian Army then liberates the Netherlands in 1944–45. Germany surrenders on May 8, 1945 (V-E Day), ending six years of war in Europe.

EVENTS.title: Liberation of the Netherlands
EVENTS.description: The Canadian Army liberates the Netherlands from Nazi occupation in 1944–45. To this day, the Dutch send 10,000 tulips to Ottawa each year as a symbol of gratitude — a tradition that began after Canadian soldiers liberated their country and sheltered the Dutch Royal Family during the war.

EVENTS.title: V-E Day: Germany Surrenders
EVENTS.description: Germany surrenders on May 8, 1945 (V-E Day), ending six years of war in Europe. Japan surrenders on August 14, 1945 — the end of four years of war in the Pacific. By the end of the Second World War, Canada had the third-largest navy in the world and had emerged as a significant middle power on the world stage.

EVENTS.title: Oil Discovered in Alberta
EVENTS.description: The discovery of oil in Alberta in 1947 begins Canada's modern energy industry. This transforms the Canadian economy — particularly western Canada — and establishes Alberta as one of the wealthiest provinces. Between 1945 and 1970, Canada enjoys one of the strongest economies among industrialized nations.

EVENTS.title: Newfoundland and Labrador Joins Canada
EVENTS.description: Newfoundland and Labrador becomes Canada's 10th province in 1949, following a close referendum vote. Prior to this it was a separate British Dominion. Its entry completes the modern map of Canada and brings with it rich fisheries, mineral resources, and the vibrant culture of its people.

EVENTS.title: Korean War
EVENTS.description: Canada participates in the UN operation defending South Korea in the Korean War (1950–53), with 500 dead and 1,000 wounded. Canada has also taken part in numerous UN peacekeeping missions in places as varied as Egypt, Cyprus and Haiti — as well as operations in the former Yugoslavia and Afghanistan.

EVENTS.title: Aboriginal Peoples Granted the Vote
EVENTS.description: Aboriginal peoples are granted the right to vote in 1960 — the last group in Canada to receive this right. Most Canadians of Asian descent had also previously been denied the vote; Japanese-Canadians were the last to regain it in 1948. Today every citizen over the age of 18 may vote.

EVENTS.title: Official Languages Act
EVENTS.description: The Official Languages Act (1969) guarantees French and English services across the federal government. It stems from the Royal Commission on Bilingualism and Biculturalism established in 1963. In 1970, Canada helps found La Francophonie — an international association of French-speaking countries.

EVENTS.title: Canada–Soviet Hockey Summit Series
EVENTS.description: Paul Henderson scores the winning goal for Canada in the Canada–Soviet Summit Series in September 1972 — often called "the goal heard around the world." It is still remembered as a defining moment in both sports and Canadian cultural identity, representing Cold War tensions and national pride.

EVENTS.title: Terry Fox: Marathon of Hope
EVENTS.description: Terry Fox, a British Columbian who lost his right leg to cancer at age 18, begins a cross-country run in 1980 — the "Marathon of Hope" — to raise money for cancer research. He becomes one of the greatest heroes in Canadian history. While he did not finish and ultimately lost his battle with cancer, his legacy continues through yearly fundraising events worldwide.

EVENTS.title: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms
EVENTS.description: The Constitution is amended in 1982 and the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms is entrenched. It begins: "Whereas Canada is founded upon principles that recognize the supremacy of God and the rule of law." Quebec does not agree to the amendment. The Charter builds on an 800-year tradition of ordered liberty dating back to Magna Carta (1215).

EVENTS.title: Rick Hansen Circles the Globe
EVENTS.description: Rick Hansen, fellow British Columbian and friend of Terry Fox, circles the globe in a wheelchair in 1985 to raise funds for spinal cord research. His "Man in Motion" world tour covers 40,000 km across 34 countries and raises over $26 million — continuing the legacy of Terry Fox.

EVENTS.title: Apology for Japanese-Canadian Internment
EVENTS.description: The Government of Canada apologises for the wartime forcible relocation of Canadians of Japanese origin and compensates victims. Despite the military and RCMP advising Ottawa they posed little danger, their property was sold without compensation. This apology is part of Canada's reckoning with historical injustices.

EVENTS.title: Nunavut Established
EVENTS.description: Nunavut — meaning "our land" in Inuktitut — is established in 1999 from the eastern part of the Northwest Territories, becoming Canada's newest and largest territory. It is a landmark achievement for Inuit self-determination and a recognition of Aboriginal land rights. Iqaluit is its capital.

EVENTS.title: Apology for the Chinese Head Tax
EVENTS.description: The Government of Canada apologises in 2006 for the Chinese Head Tax — a race-based entry fee imposed after Chinese workers helped build the Canadian Pacific Railway. The Chinese community made vital contributions to building Canada, yet faced systematic discrimination for decades afterward.

PEOPLE.name: John Cabot
PEOPLE.role: Explorer — First to Map Canada's East Coast
PEOPLE.description: Italian immigrant to England. In 1497 he becomes the first European to map Canada's Atlantic shore, setting foot on Newfoundland or Cape Breton Island and claiming the "New Founde Land" for England. English settlement does not begin until 1610.

PEOPLE.name: Jacques Cartier
PEOPLE.description: First European to explore the St. Lawrence River and set eyes on present-day Québec City and Montreal. Made three voyages (1534–1542) for King Francis I of France. He heard two guides speak the Iroquoian word kanata ("village") — by the 1550s, the name Canada begins appearing on maps.

PEOPLE.name: Pierre de Monts
PEOPLE.description: French explorer who co-founded the first European settlement north of Florida with Samuel de Champlain at Port-Royal, Acadia (present-day Nova Scotia) in 1604.

PEOPLE.name: Samuel de Champlain
PEOPLE.description: Co-founded the first European settlement at Port-Royal (1604) and built the fortress at Québec City (1608). Allied the colony with the Algonquin, Montagnais and Huron. His tireless work building alliances and exploring the continent earned him the title "Father of New France."

PEOPLE.name: Count Frontenac
PEOPLE.description: When the English demanded the surrender of Quebec in 1690, Frontenac famously replied: "My only reply will be from the mouths of my cannons!" He and leaders like Jean Talon and Bishop Laval built a French Empire in North America reaching from Hudson Bay to the Gulf of Mexico.

PEOPLE.name: Pierre Le Moyne, Sieur d'Iberville
PEOPLE.description: A great hero of New France, winning many victories over the English from James Bay in the north to Nevis in the Caribbean in the late 17th and early 18th centuries. One of the most celebrated military figures of colonial Canada.

PEOPLE.name: Brigadier James Wolfe
PEOPLE.role: British Commander at the Plains of Abraham
PEOPLE.description: British commander who led his forces to victory at the Battle of the Plains of Abraham (1759), ending France's empire in North America. Killed in the battle that he won. His victory determines that Canada will be shaped by both French and British traditions.

PEOPLE.name: Marquis de Montcalm
PEOPLE.role: French Commander at the Plains of Abraham
PEOPLE.description: French commander who defended Québec City at the Battle of the Plains of Abraham (1759). Killed in the same battle as his British opponent Wolfe. His defeat ends France's North American empire but the French-Canadian people and culture he fought for endure.

PEOPLE.name: Sir Guy Carleton (Lord Dorchester)
PEOPLE.description: As Governor of Quebec, defended the rights of the Canadiens, defeated an American military invasion of Quebec in 1775, and supervised the Loyalist migration to Nova Scotia and Quebec in 1782–83. His advocacy led to the Quebec Act of 1774, protecting French language and Catholic rights.

PEOPLE.name: Joseph Brant (Thayendanegea)
PEOPLE.description: Led thousands of Loyalist Mohawk Indians into Canada after the American Revolution. A Mohawk military and political leader who forged alliances with the British and helped shape the early settlement of Upper Canada.

PEOPLE.name: John Graves Simcoe
PEOPLE.description: Upper Canada's first Lieutenant Governor and founder of the City of York (now Toronto). Made Upper Canada the first province in the British Empire to move toward abolition of slavery (1793). A Loyalist military officer who laid the foundations of democratic and free society in English Canada.

PEOPLE.name: Mary Ann Shadd Cary
PEOPLE.description: Outspoken activist in the movement to abolish slavery in the U.S.A. In 1853 she becomes the first woman publisher in Canada, helping found and edit The Provincial Freeman — a weekly newspaper dedicated to anti-slavery, Black immigration to Canada, and temperance.

PEOPLE.name: Major-General Sir Isaac Brock
PEOPLE.description: Captured Detroit in July 1812 but was killed while defending against an American attack at Queenston Heights, near Niagara Falls — a battle the Americans lost. His partnership with Chief Tecumseh was crucial to Canada's defence. One of the most celebrated military heroes in Canadian history.

PEOPLE.name: Chief Tecumseh
PEOPLE.description: Shawnee leader who allied with British forces and Canadian volunteers to defend Canada against the American invasion. His First Nations warriors were vital to Canada's early successes in the War of 1812. Killed in battle in 1813. Celebrated as a hero by both Canadians and Indigenous peoples.

PEOPLE.name: Laura Secord
PEOPLE.description: Pioneer wife and mother of five who made a dangerous 19-mile (30 km) journey on foot to warn Lieutenant James FitzGibbon of a planned American attack in 1813. Her bravery contributed to victory at the Battle of Beaver Dams. Recognised as a Canadian heroine to this day.

PEOPLE.name: Lt.-Col. Charles de Salaberry
PEOPLE.description: Led just 460 soldiers — mostly French Canadians — to turn back 4,000 American invaders at Châteauguay in 1813. One of the most remarkable defensive victories in Canadian history and a source of enduring pride for French Canada.

PEOPLE.name: Major-General Robert Ross
PEOPLE.role: Led Expedition that Burned the White House
PEOPLE.description: Led the British expedition from Nova Scotia that burned the White House and other public buildings in Washington, D.C. in 1814, in retaliation for the American burning of York. Died in battle shortly after and was buried in Halifax with full military honours.

PEOPLE.name: Duke of Wellington
PEOPLE.role: Defender of Canada & Defeater of Napoleon
PEOPLE.description: Sent his best soldiers to defend Canada in 1814 and chose Bytown (Ottawa) as the endpoint of the Rideau Canal — directly shaping the founding of Canada's national capital. Defeated Napoleon at Waterloo in 1815. Played a pivotal role in both the defence of Canada and the reshaping of Europe.

PEOPLE.name: Sir Louis-Hippolyte La Fontaine
PEOPLE.description: Champion of French language rights and democracy. Became the first leader of a responsible government in the Canadas (1849) — meaning ministers must hold the confidence of elected representatives. Worked in partnership with Robert Baldwin to achieve this landmark reform.

PEOPLE.name: Robert Baldwin
PEOPLE.description: English-Canadian reformer who partnered with La Fontaine to work toward responsible government. His partnership with La Fontaine across the language divide became a model of bilingual cooperation and is celebrated as foundational to Canadian democracy.

PEOPLE.name: Joseph Howe
PEOPLE.description: Nova Scotia reformer who worked in parallel with La Fontaine and Baldwin toward responsible government. Nova Scotia was the first British North American colony to attain full responsible government (1847–48). A gifted orator and journalist who championed freedom of the press.

PEOPLE.name: Sir John A. Macdonald
PEOPLE.description: Born in Scotland on January 11, 1815; came to Upper Canada as a child. Lawyer in Kingston, Ontario, gifted politician and colourful personality. Canada's first Prime Minister and key architect of Confederation. January 11 is Sir John A. Macdonald Day. His portrait is on the $10 bill.

PEOPLE.name: Sir George-Étienne Cartier
PEOPLE.role: Key Architect of Confederation from Quebec
PEOPLE.description: Railway lawyer, Montrealer and close ally of Macdonald. Led Quebec into Confederation and helped negotiate the entry of the Northwest Territories, Manitoba and British Columbia into Canada. A patriotic Canadien who saw Confederation as the best way to protect French-Canadian culture.

PEOPLE.name: Sir Leonard Tilley
PEOPLE.role: Father of Confederation — Named "Dominion of Canada"
PEOPLE.description: Elected official and Father of Confederation from New Brunswick. Suggested the name "Dominion of Canada" in 1864, inspired by Psalm 72: "dominion from sea to sea." His vision of a powerful, united, free country spanning a continent is embodied in Canada's motto A Mari Usque Ad Mare.

PEOPLE.name: Sir Wilfrid Laurier
PEOPLE.description: First French-Canadian Prime Minister since Confederation. Encouraged massive immigration to the West — the railway enabled 170,000 Ukrainians, 115,000 Poles and others to settle before 1914. Believed Canada's 20th century would be great. His portrait is on the $5 bill.

PEOPLE.name: Louis Riel
PEOPLE.description: Led two Métis uprisings — 1869 (Red River) and 1885 (Saskatchewan). Elected to Parliament but never took his seat. Executed for high treason in 1885 — a decision strongly opposed in Quebec. Seen by many as a hero, a defender of Métis rights, and the father of Manitoba.

PEOPLE.name: Gabriel Dumont
PEOPLE.description: The Métis' greatest military leader during the 1885 Northwest Rebellion. A skilled hunter, marksman and tactician who served as Louis Riel's military commander. His guerrilla tactics were highly effective, though ultimately unable to overcome the forces sent by Ottawa.

PEOPLE.name: Donald Smith (Lord Strathcona)
PEOPLE.role: Drove the Last Spike of the CPR
PEOPLE.description: Scottish-born director of the Canadian Pacific Railway who drove the famous last spike on November 7, 1885, completing the railway from sea to sea. A symbol of national unity and ambition, the moment is one of the most iconic in Canadian history.

PEOPLE.name: Major-General Sir Sam Steele
PEOPLE.description: One of Canada's most colourful heroes from the ranks of the North West Mounted Police. A great frontier hero — soldier, Mountie and lawman — who served across the West and in the Boer War. Represents the spirit of the NWMP that helped build western Canada.

PEOPLE.name: Dr. Emily Stowe
PEOPLE.role: Founder of Women's Suffrage in Canada
PEOPLE.description: The first Canadian woman to practise medicine in Canada and the founder of the women's suffrage movement in Canada. Her tireless advocacy paved the way for Manitoba becoming the first province to grant women the vote in 1916, and women winning the federal vote in 1917–18.

PEOPLE.name: Sir Robert Borden
PEOPLE.description: Prime Minister during the First World War whose government gave women the right to vote in federal elections (1917) — first to nurses at the front, then to women related to men in active service. He also championed Canada's growing autonomy within the British Empire.

PEOPLE.name: Agnes Macphail
PEOPLE.description: Farmer and teacher who became the first woman elected to the House of Commons in 1921. Championed prison reform, pacifism and rural communities. A trailblazer who paved the way for women in Canadian politics.

PEOPLE.name: Thérèse Casgrain
PEOPLE.description: Campaigned relentlessly until Quebec granted women the provincial vote in 1940 — the last province to do so. Later led the CCF party in Quebec and remained active in progressive causes throughout her life. One of the most important figures in Quebec's social history.

PEOPLE.name: General Sir Arthur Currie
PEOPLE.description: A reserve officer who rose to command the Canadian Corps in WWI. Led the capture of Vimy Ridge (1917) and commanded the Hundred Days Offensive (1918) that drove Germany to surrender. Described as the greatest battlefield commander in Canadian history.

PEOPLE.name: Lt.-Col. John McCrae
PEOPLE.description: Canadian medical officer who composed the poem "In Flanders Fields" in 1915 after officiating at the funeral of a friend. The poem is one of the most quoted poems in the world and is recited every Remembrance Day. Inspired the tradition of wearing the red poppy on November 11.

PEOPLE.name: Sir Frederick Banting
PEOPLE.description: Canadian scientist at the University of Toronto who co-discovered insulin with Charles Best in 1921. Insulin is a hormone to treat diabetes that has saved over 16 million lives worldwide. Banting won the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1923.

PEOPLE.name: Charles Best
PEOPLE.description: Medical student who worked with Sir Frederick Banting to discover insulin in 1921 at the University of Toronto. One of the most important medical discoveries in history, saving tens of millions of lives from diabetes. Best continued important research throughout his career.

PEOPLE.name: Alexander Graham Bell
PEOPLE.description: Hit on the idea of the telephone at his summer house in Baddeck, Nova Scotia, Canada. While Bell is associated with the United States, his crucial inspiration came on Canadian soil — another example of Canada's contribution to world-changing technology.

PEOPLE.name: Sir Sandford Fleming
PEOPLE.description: Invented the worldwide system of standard time zones — one of the most practically important inventions of the modern era. Also an engineer who worked on the Canadian Pacific Railway and designed Canada's first postage stamp.

PEOPLE.name: James Naismith
PEOPLE.description: Canadian born in Almonte, Ontario, who invented basketball in 1891 while working at a YMCA in Springfield, Massachusetts. Basketball is now one of the world's most popular sports, played by hundreds of millions of people. Naismith is one of Canada's greatest gifts to the world of sport.

PEOPLE.name: Wayne Gretzky
PEOPLE.role: The Greatest Hockey Player of All Time
PEOPLE.description: One of the greatest hockey players of all time, known as "The Great One." Played for the Edmonton Oilers from 1979 to 1988, winning four Stanley Cups. Holds virtually every major NHL scoring record. A national icon who represents Canada's passionate relationship with ice hockey.

PEOPLE.name: Paul Henderson
PEOPLE.role: Scored "The Goal Heard Around the World"
PEOPLE.description: Scored the winning goal for Canada in the Canada–Soviet Summit Series on September 28, 1972. The goal is often called "the goal heard around the world" — still remembered as one of the most important moments in both sports and Canadian cultural history. A symbol of national pride during the Cold War.

PEOPLE.name: Terry Fox
PEOPLE.description: British Columbian who lost his right leg to cancer at the age of 18. In 1980 he began the "Marathon of Hope" — running across Canada on a prosthetic leg to raise money for cancer research. He ran 5,373 km before cancer spread to his lungs and he was forced to stop. He died in 1981. His legacy lives on in annual Terry Fox Runs worldwide.

PEOPLE.name: Rick Hansen
PEOPLE.role: Man in Motion — Wheelchair World Tour
PEOPLE.description: Fellow British Columbian and friend of Terry Fox who circled the globe in a wheelchair in 1985 to raise funds for spinal cord research. His "Man in Motion" world tour covered 40,000 km across 34 countries, raising over $26 million and inspiring people worldwide.

PEOPLE.name: Chantal Petitclerc
PEOPLE.description: World champion wheelchair racer and Paralympic gold medalist who became one of Canada's most celebrated athletes. Won 21 Paralympic medals including 14 gold medals across five Paralympic Games. Named Athlete of the Year multiple times, she is a trailblazer for Paralympic sport.

PEOPLE.name: Donovan Bailey
PEOPLE.description: In 1996 at the Atlanta Olympic Summer Games, Donovan Bailey becomes a world record sprinter and double Olympic gold medallist — winning the 100m and 4x100m relay. Born in Jamaica, he represented Canada with distinction and brought pride to the nation.

PEOPLE.name: Joseph-Armand Bombardier
PEOPLE.description: Canadian inventor who invented the snowmobile — a lightweight winter vehicle that transformed transportation in remote northern communities. His company, Bombardier, later became one of Canada's largest manufacturers, producing aircraft and transit vehicles sold worldwide.

PEOPLE.name: Reginald Fessenden
PEOPLE.description: Canadian inventor who contributed to the invention of radio, sending the first wireless voice message in the world in 1900. His work laid the foundation for modern radio broadcasting and wireless communication, making him one of the most important — and underappreciated — inventors in history.

PEOPLE.name: Mike Lazaridis
PEOPLE.description: Co-founder (with Jim Balsillie) of Research In Motion (RIM), the wireless communications company that invented the BlackBerry. The BlackBerry revolutionised mobile communication for business users worldwide in the late 1990s and 2000s, making Canada a leader in wireless technology.

PEOPLE.name: Jim Balsillie
PEOPLE.description: Co-founder of Research In Motion (RIM) with Mike Lazaridis. Together they built the BlackBerry into a global phenomenon that changed how the world communicates. Canada's most significant contribution to the smartphone era.
